title: Longest common prefix
description: Find the longest common prefix string amongst an array of strings using a Trie.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Input has 3 strings. Shortest length is 4 ("flow"). Step 2: i=0, char='f' from "flower", compare: "flow"[0]='f', "flight"[0]='f' → all match, result=['f']. Step 3: i=1, char='l' from "flower", compare: "flow"[1]='l', "flight"[1]='l' → all match, result=['f','l']. Step 4: i=2, char='o' from "flower", compare: "flow"[2]='o', "flight"[2]='i' → mismatch (o vs i), break. Step 5: Return "fl".
Step 1: Input has 3 strings. Shortest length is 3 ("dog" and "car"). Step 2: i=0, char='d' from "dog", compare: "racecar"[0]='r', mismatch immediately → break. Step 3: Return empty string "".

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Vertical Scan
approaches.summary: Compare characters at the same position across all strings until a mismatch is found or the shortest string ends. Simple and straightforward.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(S) where S is the sum of all characters (worst case)

ladders.title: L0: Full Problem — Longest Common Prefix via Vertical Scan
ladders.desc: Find the longest common prefix by comparing characters vertically across all strings.
ladders.explanation: This naive approach compares characters at each position across all strings. It stops at the first mismatch or when the shortest string ends. Time complexity is O(S) where S is the total number of characters compared. Useful for understanding the baseline before using a Trie.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input has 3 strings. Shortest length is 4 ("flow"). Step 2: i=0, char='f' from "flower", compare: "flow"[0]='f', "flight"[0]='f' → all match, result=['f']. Step 3: i=1, char='l' from "flower", compare: "flow"[1]='l', "flight"[1]='l' → all match, result=['f','l']. Step 4: i=2, char='o' from "flower", compare: "flow"[2]='o', "flight"[2]='i' → mismatch (o vs i), break. Step 5: Return "fl".
Step 1: Input has 3 strings. Shortest length is 3 ("dog" and "car"). Step 2: i=0, char='d' from "dog", compare: "racecar"[0]='r', mismatch immediately → break. Step 3: Return empty string "".

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Character at Index Across Strings
ladders.desc: Check if all strings have the same character at a given index.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the vertical scan approach. The intuition is that for a position to be part of the common prefix, every string must share the identical character at that exact index. We verify this by taking a reference character from the first string at the specified index and comparing it with the character at the same index in all other strings. If any string has a different character or is too short (index out of bounds), the check fails. The algorithm: 1) Input: list of strings strs and integer index i. 2) If strs is empty, return False (or handle as edge case). 3) Get reference char = strs[0][i] (assume i is valid for first string). 4) For each string s in strs[1:]: if i >= len(s) or s[i] != char, return False. 5) Return True if all comparisons pass. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of strings, as we iterate through each string once for the comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in a loop in the full solution to build the prefix character by character. Common mistakes include not checking index bounds for each string (leading to IndexError) and inefficiently accessing strings multiple times. Real code pattern: def chars_match_at_index(strs, i): if not strs: return False; char = strs[0][i]; return all(i < len(s) and s[i] == char for s in strs[1:]).

examples.trace: Step 1: strs has 3 strings, i=0. Step 2: Reference char = strs[0][0] = 'c'. Step 3: Compare with strs[1][0] = 'c' → match. Step 4: Compare with strs[2][0] = 'c' → match. Step 5: All match, return True.
Step 1: strs has 3 strings, i=2. Step 2: Reference char = strs[0][2] = 'p'. Step 3: Compare with strs[1][2] = 'e' → mismatch (p vs e), return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through Character Positions Vertically
ladders.desc: Loop over character indices from start to end of shortest string to build prefix.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the vertical scanning strategy. The intuition is that the common prefix is built incrementally by checking each character position in order, from left to right. We must bound the iteration by the length of the shortest string, as the common prefix cannot exceed that length. The algorithm: 1) Find min_len = minimum length among all strings. 2) Initialize an empty result list. 3) For i from 0 to min_len-1: a) Use the character comparison subroutine (from L1) to check if all strings match at index i. b) If yes, append the character to result; else break the loop. 4) Convert result list to string and return. This works because we stop at the first mismatch, ensuring we have the longest possible prefix. Time complexity is O(min_len * n) in the worst case, but since min_len is at most the length of the shortest string and we break early on mismatch, it's efficient. Space complexity is O(min_len) for storing the result. Connection to L1: This logic repeatedly calls the comparison subroutine for each index. Common mistakes include not breaking the loop on mismatch (leading to incorrect prefixes) and using string concatenation in a loop (which is O(k^2) time; use list append instead). Real code pattern: min_len = min(len(s) for s in strs); prefix_chars = []; for i in range(min_len): if chars_match_at_index(strs, i): prefix_chars.append(strs[0][i]); else: break; return ''.join(prefix_chars).

examples.trace: Step 1: min_len = 3 (shortest string "sun"). Step 2: i=0, chars_match_at_index(strs,0) checks 's' across all → True, prefix_chars=['s']. Step 3: i=1, check 'u' → True, prefix_chars=['s','u']. Step 4: i=2, check 'n' → True, prefix_chars=['s','u','n']. Step 5: i=3 not executed (min_len=3). Return "sun".

Step 1: min_len = 4 (shortest string "star"). Step 2: i=0, check 's' → True, prefix_chars=['s']. Step 3: i=1, check 't' → True, prefix_chars=['s','t']. Step 4: i=2, check 'a' → True, prefix_chars=['s','t','a']. Step 5: i=3, check 'r' → True, prefix_chars=['s','t','a','r']. Step 6: Return "star".

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum String Length in a List
ladders.desc: Determine the length of the shortest string in an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for bounding the vertical scan iteration. The intuition is that the common prefix cannot be longer than the shortest string, because if we go beyond its length, at least one string won't have a character at that index. We find the minimum length to set the maximum number of character positions we need to check. The algorithm: 1) Initialize min_len to a large value (e.g., infinity) or the length of the first string. 2) Iterate through each string in the list: a) Compute its length. b) Update min_len to the smaller of current min_len and this length. 3) Return min_len. Alternatively, use Python's min function with a generator expression. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of strings, as we examine each string once. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This min length is used as the upper bound for the loop in the core logic. Common mistakes include not handling empty list (should return 0 or handle separately) and assuming all strings are non-empty. Real code pattern: def min_string_length(strs): if not strs: return 0; min_len = float('inf'); for s in strs: min_len = min(min_len, len(s)); return min_len. Or simply: min(len(s) for s in strs) if strs else 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize min_len = inf. Step 2: For "apple", len=5, min_len=5. Step 3: For "banana", len=6, min_len=5 (since 5 < 6). Step 4: For "cherry", len=6, min_len=5. Step 5: Return 5.
Step 1: min_len = inf. Step 2: "short" len=5, min_len=5. Step 3: "longer" len=6, min_len=5. Step 4: "longest" len=7, min_len=5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Character at Index in String
ladders.desc: Retrieve a specific character from a string using its index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to string manipulation in the vertical scan. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access any character by its position (index), starting from 0 for the first character. In Python, this is done using square brackets: string[index]. It's crucial to ensure the index is within bounds (0 <= index < len(string)) to avoid IndexError. The algorithm: 1) Input: a string s and an integer index i. 2) Check if i is valid: if i < 0 or i >= len(s), handle error (e.g., return None or raise exception). 3) Return s[i]. Time complexity is O(1) for access. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when finding string lengths and in the comparison subroutine. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in some languages) or not checking bounds leading to runtime errors. Real code pattern: def get_char(s, i): if 0 <= i < len(s): return s[i]; else: return None (or raise IndexError).

examples.trace: Step 1: s = "hello", len=5. Step 2: i=1 is valid (0 <= 1 < 5). Step 3: Return s[1] which is 'e'.
Step 1: s = "world", len=5. Step 2: i=4 is valid (0 <= 4 < 5). Step 3: Return s[4] which is 'd'.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences of Characters
ladders.desc: Basic understanding that strings are ordered sequences of characters with length and indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is prerequisite for any string manipulation algorithm. The intuition is that a string is not just a blob of text but a sequence where each character has a specific position. In programming, strings are typically zero-indexed: the first character is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. For example, "cat" has length 3 with characters 'c' at index 0, 'a' at index 1, 't' at index 2. This ordering allows us to compare characters across strings by their positions. Key operations include: getting length (len(s)), accessing character at index (s[i]), and iterating through characters. Time complexity for length and access is O(1) in most languages. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the string. Connection to L4: This concept enables the character access operation. Common mistakes include confusion between indices and positions (e.g., thinking index 1 means first character) and not accounting for empty strings (length 0). Real code pattern: In Python, s = "example"; length = len(s); first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[length-1]; iterate with for char in s: or for i in range(len(s)): s[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: String "dog" has 3 characters. Step 2: Index 0 corresponds to first character 'd'. Step 3: Index 1 corresponds to second character 'o'. Step 4: Index 2 corresponds to third character 'g'. Step 5: No index 3 as length is 3.
Step 1: Empty string has no characters. Step 2: Length is 0. Step 3: There are no valid indices (0, 1, etc.) because the string is empty.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Trie-Based Longest Common Prefix
approaches.summary: Insert all strings into a Trie, then traverse from the root while each node has exactly one child (no branching). The path traversed is the longest common prefix.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(S) for building Trie + O(L) for traversal

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Prefix Using Trie
ladders.desc: Complete solution: Build a trie from all strings, then traverse to find the longest path where each node has only one child.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that a trie (prefix tree) naturally organizes strings by their common prefixes. By inserting all strings into a trie, we create a structure where shared prefixes merge into single paths. The longest common prefix corresponds to the deepest path from the root where every node has exactly one child—meaning all strings share that character sequence. If we encounter a node with multiple children or that marks the end of a word, we stop, as the commonality ends there.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define a TrieNode class with children (dictionary) and is_end flag.
2. Insert each string into the trie: For each character, create a new node if needed, move to that node, mark is_end at the last character.
3. Find the longest common prefix: Start at the root, traverse while the current node has exactly one child and is not an end node. Append the character to the result.
4. Return the accumulated prefix.

Time complexity: O(S) for building the trie (S = total characters in all strings) + O(L) for traversal (L = length of the common prefix). Space complexity: O(S) in worst case to store all nodes.

Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the trie building (L1) and prefix search (L2) into a complete workflow.

Common mistakes: Not handling empty input, forgetting to stop at word ends, or incorrectly counting children. Always check for edge cases like single string or no common prefix.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
class TrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = {}
        self.is_end = False

def longest_common_prefix(strs):
    if not strs: return ''
    root = TrieNode()
    for s in strs:
        node = root
        for ch in s:
            if ch not in node.children:
                node.children[ch] = TrieNode()
            node = node.children[ch]
        node.is_end = True
    result = []
    node = root
    while len(node.children) == 1 and not node.is_end:
        ch = list(node.children.keys())[0]
        result.append(ch)
        node = node.children[ch]
    return ''.join(result)

examples.trace: Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'flower': root->f->l->o->w->e->r (is_end=True). Insert 'flow': root->f->l->o->w (is_end=True, shares 'flo' path). Insert 'flight': root->f->l->i->g->h->t (is_end=True, shares 'fl' path). Step 2: Traverse from root. Root has one child 'f', append 'f'. Node 'f' has one child 'l', append 'l'. Node 'l' has two children ('o' and 'i'), stop. Result: 'fl'.

Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'dog': root->d->o->g (is_end=True). Insert 'racecar': root->r->a->c->e->c->a->r (is_end=True). Insert 'car': root->c->a->r (is_end=True). Step 2: Traverse from root. Root has three children ('d','r','c'), stop immediately. Result: empty string.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building the Trie from Strings
ladders.desc: Insert multiple strings into a trie data structure.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition is that a trie efficiently stores strings by sharing common prefixes in a tree structure. Each node represents a character, and paths from the root spell out strings. By inserting all input strings, we create a merged representation where overlapping prefixes are stored once, saving space and enabling fast prefix queries.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize a root TrieNode with children dictionary and is_end flag.
2. For each string in the input list:
   a. Start at the root node.
   b. For each character in the string:
      i. If the character is not in the current node's children, create a new TrieNode.
      ii. Move to that child node.
   c. After processing all characters, mark the current node's is_end as True.

Time complexity: O(S) where S is the total number of characters across all strings. Each character is processed once. Space complexity: O(S) in worst case if no prefixes are shared, but typically less due to sharing.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is the first half of the full solution, creating the trie that will be traversed to find the common prefix.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark word ends, which can cause incorrect traversal later. Also, not handling empty strings properly—they should be inserted as a special case or skipped.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
class TrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = {}
        self.is_end = False

def build_trie(strs):
    root = TrieNode()
    for s in strs:
        node = root
        for ch in s:
            if ch not in node.children:
                node.children[ch] = TrieNode()
            node = node.children[ch]
        node.is_end = True
    return root

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with root node. Insert 'cat': root->c->a->t, mark 't' node is_end=True. Step 2: Insert 'car': root->c (exists)->a (exists)->r (new node), mark 'r' node is_end=True. Final trie: root has child 'c', which has child 'a', which has children 't' and 'r', both marked is_end.

Step 1: Insert 'hello': root->h->e->l->l->o, mark 'o' is_end=True. Step 2: Insert 'hi': root->h (exists)->i (new node), mark 'i' is_end=True. Final trie: root has child 'h', which has children 'e' and 'i'. 'e' leads to 'l->l->o' path.

testCases.expected: root->a->b->c and empty string not inserted as path
root->1->2->3 with is_end at each number end
root->a->p->p->l->e and is_end at second 'p' and 'e'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversing Trie to Find Common Prefix
ladders.desc: Starting from the root, traverse down while nodes have exactly one child and are not word ends.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition is that in a trie containing multiple strings, the longest common prefix corresponds to the longest path from the root where every node has only one child—indicating all strings share that character. If a node has multiple children, strings diverge there. If a node marks the end of a word, some string ends there, so the common prefix cannot extend further.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start at the root node of the trie.
2. Initialize an empty result string.
3. While the current node has exactly one child and is not an end node (is_end is False):
   a. Get the single child character.
   b. Append it to the result.
   c. Move to that child node.
4. Return the result.

Time complexity: O(L) where L is the length of the common prefix, as we traverse one node per character. Space complexity: O(1) extra space.

Connection to L1: This logic uses the trie built in L1 to efficiently find the common prefix without comparing strings directly.

Common mistakes: Not checking is_end condition, which can overextend the prefix if a word ends earlier. Also, incorrectly handling the root—it should not contribute a character.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def find_common_prefix(root):
    result = []
    node = root
    while len(node.children) == 1 and not node.is_end:
        ch = list(node.children.keys())[0]
        result.append(ch)
        node = node.children[ch]
    return ''.join(result)

examples.trace: Start at root. Root has one child 'f', is_end=False, append 'f', move to 'f'. Node 'f' has one child 'l', is_end=False, append 'l', move to 'l'. Node 'l' has two children ('o' and 'i'), stop. Result: 'fl'.

Start at root. Root has one child 'h', append 'h', move to 'h'. Node 'h' has one child 'e', append 'e', move to 'e'. Node 'e' has one child 'l', append 'l', move to first 'l'. Node first 'l' has one child 'l', append 'l', move to second 'l'. Node second 'l' has one child 'o' and is_end=True (for 'hell'), stop. Result: 'hell'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure and Operations
ladders.desc: Implement a trie node with children mapping and end marker, supporting insert and search.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition is that a trie is a tree-like data structure where each node represents a single character of a string. Nodes are connected to form paths that spell out words. By storing children in a dictionary (or array), we enable fast lookups. The is_end flag marks where a complete word ends, which is crucial for distinguishing prefixes from full words.

Step-by-step operations:
1. Node structure: Each TrieNode has a children dictionary (key: character, value: TrieNode) and is_end boolean.
2. Insert a string: Traverse from root, creating nodes as needed, set is_end at the last node.
3. Search a string: Traverse from root, following characters; return True if all characters found and last node has is_end=True.

Time complexity: O(L) for insert or search, where L is string length. Space complexity: O(N*L) in worst case for N strings of length L, but shared prefixes reduce this.

Connection to L2: This building block provides the node class and basic operations that are used to build the trie in L1 and traverse it in L2.

Common mistakes: Using a list for children (inefficient for large alphabets), forgetting to initialize is_end as False, or not handling duplicate inserts gracefully.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
class TrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = {}  # char -> TrieNode
        self.is_end = False

def insert(root, word):
    node = root
    for ch in word:
        if ch not in node.children:
            node.children[ch] = TrieNode()
        node = node.children[ch]
    node.is_end = True

def search(root, word):
    node = root
    for ch in word:
        if ch not in node.children:
            return False
        node = node.children[ch]
    return node.is_end

examples.input: Insert 'cat' into empty trie, then search 'cat'
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize root. Insert 'cat': root.children['c']=new node, move to 'c'; 'c'.children['a']=new node, move to 'a'; 'a'.children['t']=new node, move to 't'; set 't'.is_end=True. Step 2: Search 'cat': start at root, follow 'c'->'a'->'t', check 't'.is_end=True, return True.
Step 1: Insert 'do': root->d->o, set 'o'.is_end=True. Step 2: Search 'dog': start at root, follow 'd'->'o', but 'o'.children does not have 'g', return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Inserting a Single String into Trie
ladders.desc: Add one string to a trie by creating nodes for each character.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition is that inserting a string into a trie involves breaking it down into characters and creating a chain of nodes from the root. Each node represents a character, and the path from the root to a node spells out a prefix. If nodes already exist for shared prefixes, we reuse them, making the trie space-efficient. The is_end marker at the final node indicates that this path represents a complete word.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start at the root node.
2. For each character in the string:
   a. Check if the current node's children dictionary contains this character.
   b. If not, create a new TrieNode and add it to children.
   c. Move to the child node corresponding to the character.
3. After processing all characters, set the current node's is_end to True.

Time complexity: O(L) where L is the length of the string, as we process each character once. Space complexity: O(L) in worst case if no prefixes are shared, but typically less.

Connection to L3: This operation is a specific use of the TrieNode structure, implementing the insert function that builds up the trie.

Common mistakes: Not moving to the child node correctly, forgetting to set is_end, or inserting duplicate strings without handling them (they should be idempotent).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def insert_string(root, s):
    node = root
    for ch in s:
        if ch not in node.children:
            node.children[ch] = TrieNode()
        node = node.children[ch]
    node.is_end = True

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. For 't': not in children, create node, move to 't'. Step 2: For 'r': not in 't'.children, create node, move to 'r'. Step 3: For 'e': not in 'r'.children, create node, move to first 'e'. Step 4: For 'e': not in first 'e'.children, create node, move to second 'e'. Set second 'e'.is_end=True.

examples.input: Insert 'an' into trie with 'ant' already inserted
examples.trace: Step 1: Trie has root->a->n->t from 'ant'. Step 2: Insert 'an': start at root, 'a' exists, move to 'a'; 'n' exists, move to 'n'; set 'n'.is_end=True (now 'n' marks end of 'an' and continues to 't' for 'ant').

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Prefix Trees (Tries)
ladders.desc: Understanding how tries organize strings by shared prefixes for efficient common prefix finding.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind a trie (pronounced 'try', from retrieval) is that it's a tree-like data structure designed to store a dynamic set of strings, where keys are usually strings. It's called a prefix tree because it organizes data by common prefixes—strings that share the same starting characters will share nodes in the trie. This makes it extremely efficient for operations like searching for prefixes, autocomplete, and spell checking.

Key concepts:
1. Structure: Each node in a trie represents a single character. The root represents an empty string. Children pointers lead to the next characters.
2. Paths: A path from the root to any node spells out a prefix or full string. For example, if we insert 'cat', we have a path root->c->a->t.
3. Sharing: If we then insert 'car', we reuse the 'c' and 'a' nodes, only adding a new 'r' node. This saves space.
4. End marker: Nodes often have a boolean flag (like is_end) to indicate whether the path from the root to that node forms a complete word in the set.

Why it's useful: For the longest common prefix problem, a trie allows us to find the shared prefix without comparing each string pairwise. We just traverse from the root as long as there's only one child, which means all strings have that character in that position.

Simple analogy: Think of a trie like a family tree for words. The root is the common ancestor, and each branch represents a different starting letter. Words that start the same share great-grandparents.

Time/space basics: Inserting a string of length L takes O(L) time. Space depends on how many unique prefixes there are—in worst case, if no strings share prefixes, it's O(total characters).

Connection to L4: This foundation explains why we insert strings character by character and how the structure enables efficient prefix operations.

Common misunderstandings: Tries are not the same as binary search trees; they can have many children per node. Also, they're not always the best for small datasets due to overhead.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
# Basic idea without full implementation
class SimpleTrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = {}  # Stores next characters
        self.is_word = False  # Marks end of a word

# To store 'hi': root.children['h'] = node1, node1.children['i'] = node2, node2.is_word = True

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 'to': root->t->o, mark 'o' as word end. Step 2: Insert 'tea': root->t (exists)->e (new)->a (new), mark 'a' as word end. Step 3: Insert 'ted': root->t (exists)->e (exists)->d (new), mark 'd' as word end. Final trie: root has child 't', which has children 'o' and 'e'. 'e' has children 'a' and 'd'.

examples.input: How does trie help find common prefix of ['fun','funny','function']?
examples.trace: After inserting all words, the trie has root->f->u->n. From 'n', it branches to 'n' for 'funny', 'c' for 'function', etc. To find common prefix, start at root: only child 'f', then 'u', then 'n'—all have one child until 'n'. So common prefix is 'fun'.

testCases.input: What is the root of a trie?
If trie has 'be', does it contain 'b'?
testCases.expected: Only if 'b' was inserted as a word

testCases.input: Insert 'go', then 'gone'. How many nodes for 'go'?

testCases.expected: Marks end of a complete word

testCases.input: Why is trie good for prefixes?
testCases.expected: Strings with same prefix share nodes

testCases.input: What is time to insert string length n?
What is a disadvantage of trie?
testCases.expected: Can use more memory than alternatives

approaches.name: Approach 3: Divide and Conquer (Horizontal Scan)
approaches.summary: Recursively split the array into halves, compute LCP for each half, then combine the two LCPs. Theoretical divide-and-conquer strategy.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(S) with O(log n) recursion depth
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(L log n) for recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Common Prefix using Divide and Conquer Horizontal Scan
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding longest common prefix by recursively dividing strings and combining results.
ladders.explanation: This approach uses a divide-and-conquer strategy to find the longest common prefix among an array of strings. The intuition is to break down the problem into smaller subproblems: instead of comparing all strings at once, we recursively split the array into halves, find the LCP for each half, and then combine the two prefixes by comparing them character by character. This method leverages the property that the LCP of the entire array equals the common prefix between the LCPs of its two halves. While this approach has theoretical parallelism benefits, in practice it has similar O(S) time complexity to brute force (where S is the sum of all characters in all strings) due to the need to compare characters across all strings in worst-case scenarios. The space complexity is O(m * log n) for the recursion stack, where m is the length of the prefix and n is the number of strings. The algorithm works by: 1) Handling base cases (empty array, single string). 2) Recursively computing LCP for left and right halves. 3) Combining results by finding the common prefix between the two computed prefixes. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty arrays or strings of varying lengths properly, and inefficient character-by-character comparison that doesn't short-circuit early. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the key subroutine of comparing two strings to find their common prefix.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array has 3 strings. Split into left half ["flower", "flow"] and right half ["flight"]. Step 2: For left half: Split into ["flower"] and ["flow"]. LCP of ["flower"] is "flower", LCP of ["flow"] is "flow". Combine: Compare "flower" and "flow" character by character: 'f' matches, 'l' matches, 'o' vs 'o' matches, 'w' vs 'w' matches, then end of "flow", so LCP is "flow". Step 3: For right half ["flight"], LCP is "flight". Step 4: Combine left LCP "flow" and right LCP "flight": Compare 'f' matches, 'l' matches, 'o' vs 'i' doesn't match, so LCP is "fl". Final output: "fl".

Step 1: Input array has 3 strings. Split into left half ["dog", "racecar"] and right half ["car"]. Step 2: For left half: Split into ["dog"] and ["racecar"]. LCP of ["dog"] is "dog", LCP of ["racecar"] is "racecar". Combine: Compare "dog" and "racecar": 'd' vs 'r' doesn't match, so LCP is "". Step 3: For right half ["car"], LCP is "car". Step 4: Combine left LCP "" and right LCP "car": Empty string, so LCP is "". Final output: "".

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Two Strings to Find Common Prefix
ladders.desc: Helper function that takes two strings and returns their longest common prefix.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core combining operation in the divide-and-conquer approach. The intuition is simple: to find what two strings share at the beginning, we compare them character by character from the start until we find a mismatch or reach the end of the shorter string. This works because a common prefix must be contiguous from the first character. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty result string. 2) Iterate through indices from 0 to min(length of string1, length of string2)-1. 3) At each index, if characters match, append to result; otherwise break. 4) Return the accumulated result. Time complexity is O(min(m, n)) where m and n are the lengths of the two strings. Space complexity is O(min(m, n)) for the result string. This feeds into L0 by being the operation that combines LCPs from two halves. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings correctly (should return empty string immediately) or continuing comparison beyond the shorter string's length. The real code pattern involves a simple loop with early termination.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare characters at index 0: 'f' == 'f', add 'f'. Step 2: Index 1: 'l' == 'l', add 'l'. Step 3: Index 2: 'o' == 'o', add 'o'. Step 4: Index 3: 'w' == 'w', add 'w'. Step 5: Index 4: s1 has 'e', s2 has ended (length 4), so stop. Result: "flow".
Step 1: Index 0: 'h' == 'h', add 'h'. Step 2: Index 1: 'e' == 'e', add 'e'. Step 3: Index 2: 'l' vs 'y' don't match, break. Result: "he".

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Divide and Combine for Prefix Finding
ladders.desc: The recursive strategy of splitting array and merging LCP results.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the divide-and-conquer logic without full implementation details. The intuition is that the LCP of an entire array can be found by recursively finding LCPs of smaller subarrays and combining them. This works because the LCP operation is associative: LCP(strs) = LCP(LCP(left_half), LCP(right_half)). By dividing the problem, we reduce the number of direct string comparisons in theory, though worst-case time remains O(S). The algorithm: 1) Base case: if array is empty, return empty string; if single element, return that string. 2) Recursive case: split array into two halves (mid = n//2). 3) Recursively compute lcp_left for left half and lcp_right for right half. 4) Combine using the compare-two-strings subroutine from L1. Time complexity: O(S) in worst case as each character might be compared, with O(log n) recursion depth. Space: O(m * log n) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 as the main algorithmic structure. Common mistakes include incorrect splitting indices or not handling base cases properly. The real pattern involves a recursive function with divide-and-conquer structure.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array size 3 > 1, split at mid=1: left = ["ab"], right = ["abc", "abcd"]. Step 2: Left half ["ab"]: base case, return "ab". Step 3: Right half ["abc", "abcd"]: split: left2 = ["abc"], right2 = ["abcd"]. Left2 returns "abc", right2 returns "abcd". Combine "abc" and "abcd": compare 'a','b','c' vs 'd' at index 2, mismatch, so "ab". Step 4: Combine left "ab" and right "ab": compare fully, return "ab".

Step 1: Split: left = ["x"], right = ["y", "z"]. Step 2: Left returns "x". Step 3: Right: split into ["y"] and ["z"], returns "y" and "z", combine gives "" (no common prefix). Step 4: Combine "x" and "" gives "".

ladders.title: Building Block — String Array Splitting and Recursion Base Cases
ladders.desc: How to properly split an array and handle recursion termination conditions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two key parts: splitting an array into halves for divide-and-conquer, and defining base cases to stop recursion. The intuition is that to apply divide-and-conquer, we need to break the problem into smaller, manageable pieces, and know when to stop breaking it down. Splitting works by finding the middle index (using integer division) to create left and right subarrays. Base cases handle scenarios where no further division is needed: empty array (return empty string) or single element (return that string as its own LCP). This ensures the recursion terminates correctly. The algorithm: 1) Check if array is empty: return "". 2) Check if array has one element: return that string. 3) Otherwise, compute mid = len(strs)//2. 4) Create left_half = strs[0:mid], right_half = strs[mid:]. Time complexity for splitting is O(1) for index calculation, but array slicing may be O(k) where k is subarray size depending on language. Space complexity involves O(n) for subarrays in worst case. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to create subproblems. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in splitting or forgetting base cases. The real pattern uses conditional checks and array slicing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 4 elements. Step 2: mid = 4//2 = 2. Step 3: left_half = strs[0:2] = ["a", "b"]. Step 4: right_half = strs[2:4] = ["c", "d"].
Step 1: Array has 1 element. Step 2: Base case triggered, return the string "single" without splitting.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character-by-Character String Comparison
ladders.desc: Iterating through two strings and comparing characters at each position.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves looping through two strings simultaneously and checking if characters match. The intuition is that to find commonality, we need to examine each position from the start. This works because strings are sequences of characters, and we can access them by index. The algorithm: 1) Determine the minimum length of the two strings to avoid index errors. 2) Use a loop from 0 to min_length-1. 3) In each iteration, compare characters at current index. 4) If they match, continue; if not, break the loop. 5) The result is the substring from start to the last matching index. Time complexity is O(min(m, n)) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1) if not building a new string, or O(min(m, n)) if storing result. This feeds into L1 as the core of comparing two strings. Common mistakes include comparing beyond string lengths or not handling empty strings. The real pattern uses a for loop with range and direct character access.

examples.output: Match until index 1, mismatch at index 2
examples.trace: Step 1: min length = 3. Step 2: Index 0: 'a' == 'a', match. Step 3: Index 1: 'b' == 'b', match. Step 4: Index 2: 'c' vs 'd', mismatch, break.
Step 1: min length = 4. Step 2: Index 0: 't' == 't'. Step 3: Index 1: 'e' == 'e'. Step 4: Index 2: 's' == 's'. Step 5: Index 3: 't' == 't'. All match.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Divide and Conquer Strategy
ladders.desc: Understanding how breaking a problem into smaller subproblems and combining results works.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a prefix of a string is a substring that starts at the beginning of the string and goes up to any position. For example, in the string "hello", the prefixes are "", "h", "he", "hel", "hell", "hello". The intuition is that a prefix is like the first part of a string, and it must be contiguous from the start. This works because strings are ordered sequences of characters. The longest common prefix among multiple strings is the longest string that is a prefix of all given strings. For instance, for ["flower", "flow", "flight"], the common prefixes are "", "f", "fl", and "fl" is the longest. Time and space analysis are not applicable here as it's a definition. This feeds into L4 by establishing what we're trying to find when comparing characters. Common mistakes include confusing prefix with substring (which can start anywhere) or suffix (which ends at the end). The real pattern is just understanding string indexing from position 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at beginning. Step 2: Prefix of length 0: "". Step 3: Length 1: "a". Step 4: Length 2: "ap". Step 5: Length 3: "app". Step 6: Length 4: "appl". Step 7: Length 5: "apple".

Step 1: Compare from start: "" is always common. Step 2: First character: 'c' matches, so "c". Step 3: Second character: 'a' matches, so "ca". Step 4: Third character: 't' vs 'r' don't match, so stop. Common prefixes are "", "c", "ca".

comparison.content:
### Approach Comparison for Longest Common Prefix

| Approach                        | Time Complexity     | Space Complexity       | Best For                              | Drawbacks                              |
|--------------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Brute Force Vertical Scan      | O(S)               | O(1)                   | Small number of strings               | Inefficient for many/long strings      |
| Trie-Based                     | O(S) build + O(L)   | O(S)                   | Multiple queries or dynamic strings   | Higher memory usage                    |
| Divide & Conquer               | O(S)               | O(L log n) recursion   | Learning recursive techniques         | More complex, similar time to brute    |

**Key Takeaways:**
- **Trie** is excellent when you need to support insertions and multiple prefix-related queries.
- **Brute Force** is simple and sufficient for small inputs.
- **Divide & Conquer** is more of a theoretical exercise for this problem.
